coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot crosslinking towards mechanochemically and photochemically induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Wenli
Xiang, Shishuai
Bai, Minghao
Ruan, Yonghong
Zheng, Jinli
Cao, Xuezheng
Xu, Yuanze
Chen, Yinjun
Weng, Wengui
coredata.dc:description: Mechanochromism in polymers has attracted great interests in recent years. Herein, we develop a mechanochromic elastomer carrying spiropyran mechanophores in the chain and mechanoresponsiveness of elastomer is enhanced by embedding luminescent carbon dot (CD) nanoparticles as crosslinkers and energy donor in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Compared with the control with physically blended CD, CD crosslinked elastomer exhibit excellent daylight and fluorescent colors change under stretching. The mechanochemically induced fluorescence change is a result of FRET between CD nanoparticles and the activated merocyanine. The control with physically blended CD nanoparticles shows low FRET in stretching due to lower fraction of spiropyran activation as a result of lower mechanical properties. Furthermore, the CD crosslinked elastomer has already been applied in rewritable mechanochemical and photochemical recording or printing. Our demonstration shows that interfacial chemical bonding plays a critical role in the reinforcement of mechanical properties for polymer composites and may exhibit great potential in the study of the acceleration of mechanoresponsiveness of polymer and multi-responsiveness of polymer nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mechanochromism
Luminescent carbon dot nanofiller
Nanocomposites
Energy donor
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CD crosslinked polyurethane elastomer
Characteristic of luminescent CD nanoparticles and SP mechanophore

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) size distribution histograms of CD nanoparticles.
DMA analysis (a) and stress-strain curves (b) of PSP×CD and PSP + CD elastomers.
(a) Images of PSP×CD and (b) PSP + CD samples at different strains (stretch ratios of 0%–500% and 0%–1100%, respectively) during uniaxial tensile test.

Fluorescent excitation spectrum of SP after UV activation and fluorescent emission spectrum of CDs. (b) Images of PSP×CD sample at different strains (stretch ratios of 0%–500%) under the irradiation of UV during uniaxial extension.

(a) Photographs of PSP×CD and PSP + CD under daylight and 418 nm light before and after UV treatment. (b) Fluorescent emission spectra of PSP×CD and PSP + CD before and after mechanical activation/UV irradiation.
The study of PSP×CD in rewritable mechanochemical and photochemical recording and printing.
Schematic illustration of the CD crosslinked elastomers with mechanochemically/photochemically regulated FRET.
The synthetic route of mechanoresponsive polyurethane crosslinking by CD nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot crosslinking towards mechanochemically and photochemically induced fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mechanochromism in polymers has attracted great interests in recent years. Herein, we develop a mechanochromic elastomer carrying spiropyran mechanophores in the chain and mechanoresponsiveness of elastomer is enhanced by embedding luminescent carbon dot (CD) nanoparticles as crosslinkers and energy donor in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Compared with the control with physically blended CD, CD crosslinked elastomer exhibit excellent daylight and fluorescent colors change under stretching. The mechanochemically induced fluorescence change is a result of FRET between CD nanoparticles and the activated merocyanine. The control with physically blended CD nanoparticles shows low FRET in stretching due to lower fraction of spiropyran activation as a result of lower mechanical properties. Furthermore, the CD crosslinked elastomer has already been applied in rewritable mechanochemical and photochemical recording or printing. Our demonstration shows that interfacial chemical bonding plays a critical role in the reinforcement of mechanical properties for polymer composites and may exhibit great potential in the study of the acceleration of mechanoresponsiveness of polymer and multi-responsiveness of polymer nanocomposites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer mechanochemistry has attracted considerable attention in both polymer and chemistry fields in the last decade [1–3]. The mechanochemical change of a mechanically sensitive moiety (mechanophore) embedded into the polymer chain enables a series of interesting features such as changes in color [4,5], fluorescence [6], stiffness [5], etc. In particular, mechanochromism shows potentiality of important applications in stress sensing and damage prevention [7–9]. A wide variety of mechanochromic polymers have been created with a good number of mechanophores including spiropyrans [10–18], spirothiopyran [19,20], naphthopyrans [21–23], oxazines [24], spirooxazine [25], rhodamines [26–29] and radical-type ones [30–33]. In order to achieve enhanced sensitivity, various strategies have been conducted to reinforce mechanical properties of polymers and further improve the mechanical response of materials, including the incorporation of supramolecular interactions [11,34], covalent filler networks and nanofillers as sacrificial bonds [35,36]. A typical example was reported by Sottos et al., who modified spiropyran mechanophore at the interface between nanoparticles and polymer matrix to achieve the greater level of spiropyran activation in nanocomposites compared with in bulk polymers [37]. In a similar manner, Otsuka and coworkers crosslinked diarylbibenzofuranone based radical-type mechanophore on the surface of cellulose nanocrystals and silica networks, and the mechanophore displayed high sensitivity of mechanical stress due to the effective stress transmission between nanoparticles [38–40].

In addition, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) as a new approach was performed to enhance mechanical response [41]. For example, Chen and coworkers utilized fluorescent conjugated microporous polymer nanofillers as energy accepters to absorb the mechanically induced blue light from dioxetane mechanophore and improved the sensitivity of fluorescent detection [42]. However, polymer materials with higher intensity of mechanical response are expected to meet a greater sensitivity and resolution as stress or strain sensor.

Prospectively, a pathway to further manipulate mechanical response of polymer materials is combining nanocomposites and FRET strategies in the architecture of mechanoresponsive polymers. Nanofillers reinforce the mechanical properties and further mechanical response of materials. Meanwhile, mechanical response is improved by FRET. The combination of nanocomposites and FRET enables synergistic action to accelerate the mechanical activation of mechanophores. On the other hand, carbon dot (CD) nanoparticles, which are generally used as bactericide [43–46], bioimaging [47–49], photocatalyst [50,51], can also be potentially used to improve fluorescent intensity of polymer materials based on FRET mechanism [52–55]. Accordingly, embedding CD nanoparticles not only can reinforce the mechanical properties of polymer nanocomposites as nanofillers, but also can tailor the fluorescence of materials as energy donor in FRET, leading to synergistic enhancement of mechanoresponsiveness of polymer materials.

Here, we take a strategy by introducing luminescent CD nanoparticles as crosslinker and energy donor to improve mechanical response of polymer materials. CD nanoparticles chemically crosslinked the polyurethane elastomers with spiropyran (SP) mechanophores in the chains as shown in Scheme 1 . Upon activation of SP into merocyanine (MC) either by force or UV, color change and FRET from CD to MC in the elastomers were explored and the influence of crosslinking between CD and polymer matrix on mechanochromism was studied as well.

All reagents and solvents were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification unless otherwise stated. The UV–Vis spectra were performed on Carry 5000 and fluorescence spectra on Edinburgh FLS980 and F7000. NMR spectra were recorded by 500 MHz Bruker Advance III spectrometer at 25 °C. Mechanical tests were performed on an Instron 3343 instrument with an initial strain rate of 0.05 s−1. Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was conducted by TA Instruments DMA 850 at a heating rate of 4 °C/min. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was performed using JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at 200 kV.

CD nanoparticles were prepared according to the method (Scheme S1) reported previously [56]. In brief, 10.0 mL hydroquinone solution (0.1 M) was added into a 50 mL flask followed by 0.5 mL ethylenediamine (1.0 M). The solution was mixed thoroughly with ultrasonication and then heated to 50 °C for 2 h. The solution was purified by silica-gel column chromatography (eluate, ethyl acetate/n-hexane = 3:2). The solvent was removed under vacuum to provide solid CD nanoparticles in yellow. Finally, synthesized CD nanoparticles were dissolved in ultra-dry dichloromethane (DCM) with a concentration of 2 mg/mL for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD crosslinked polyurethane elastomer
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic route of polymer materials is shown in Scheme 2 . Firstly, spiropyran (SP) diol was synthesized (In ESI). Scheme S2 [16,57] shows the synthetic route of SP diol. Then, as-synthesized SP diol (30 mg, 0.078 mmol), anhydrous polytetrahydrofuran glycol (PTMG) (Mn = 2000 g/mol, 1.62 g, 0.81 mmol), 1,6-hexyldisiocyanate (HDI) 272 μL, 1.70 mmol), and dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL) (5 μL) catalyst were mixed with 4.0 mL dichloromethane in a 25 mL flask under N2 atmosphere. The mixture was reacted at 40 °C for 1.5 h to get SP containing polyurethane prepolymer. Then 2.0 mL CD solution was injected into the prepolymer solution for cross-linking reaction. After 3 h, the viscous polymer solution was poured into a Teflon mold, centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 3 min to remove air bubbles and dried overnight at room temperature. The residual solvent was gradually removed in vacuum at 35 °C for 24 h and a flat polymer film was afforded, denoted as PSP×CD.

SP diol (27.8 mg, 0.0728 mmol), anhydrous PTMG (Mn = 2000 g/mol, 1.62 g, 0.81 mmol), HDI (148.1 mg, 0.882 mmol), and DBTDL (5 μL) were dissolved in dichloromethane (4.0 mL) and the solution polymerized at 40 °C under N2 atmosphere for 5 h to get SP containing polyurethane. After polymerization was done, 1.85 mL CD solution was poured into the SP containing polyurethane solution and formed a homogeneous polymer composite solution. The solution was then casted into a Teflon mold, centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 3 min to remove air bubbles and dried overnight at room temperature. The residual solvent in polymer material was completely evaporated in vacuum at 35 °C for 24 h to provide a control polymer film, labelled as PSP + CD. Note that CD nanoparticles almost do not covalently link to polymer matrix in PSP + CD sample due to the identical stoichiometric amount of isocyanate and hydroxyl group in polymerization.

In addition, to investigate the influence of SP concentration on FRET, another group of samples were prepared by reducing SP concentration to 1% of PSP×CD sample. The new group of samples were afforded by performing the same polymerized procedure as PSP×CD and PSP + CD with a lower SP concentration. The contents of SP and CD used in polymerization for the two groups of elastomers were listed in Table S1 and the detail of fabrication of polymers can be found in ESI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic of luminescent CD nanoparticles and SP mechanophore
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent CD nanoparticles were obtained through a one-step reaction between ethylenediamine and hydroquinone (Scheme S1) as literature reported [56]. TEM image in Fig. 1 shows that the synthesized CD nanoparticles present an average diameter of below 10 nm. SP diol was synthesized according to previous method (Scheme S2，Figure S1-S6) [17,57]. Upon irradiation with UV light, the non-emissive SP form underwent reversible photoisomerization to the MC state (absorption band at 560 nm) with a fluorescent emission peak at 660 nm (Figure S7). The purple MC solution showed slight purplish red fluorescence under the irradiation of 418 nm light (See Figure S7a). Additionally, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra of the synthesized CDs dispersed in dichloromethane were given in Figure S8, which showed that the CDs exhibited strong absorption at around 400 nm and a bright emission centered at ca. 475 nm with an excitation wavelength of 418 nm.

DMA (Fig. 2 a) shows a similar glass transition temperature of about −51 °C for PSP×CD and PSP + CD, but higher moduli for PSP×CD than PSP + CD throughout the testing temperature range. Uniaxial tests on an Instron machine were also carried out to study the mechanical performances of PSP×CD and the control PSP + CD. Fig. 2b shows the stress-strain curves of two elastomers of PSP×CD and PSP + CD. Compared with PSP + CD sample, mechanical properties of PSP×CD is significantly reinforced by embedding CD nanoparticles. Despite that PSP×CD exhibits a lower strain at break, it presents much higher Young's modulus, tensile strength and fracture toughness than those of PSP + CD (See Table S1). These results confirm successful interfacial crosslinking between CDs and polymer matrix, and its consequent beneficial effects on mechanical and thermal performances. During the tensile process, both PSP×CD (Fig. 3 a) and PSP + CD (Fig. 3b) turned from pale yellow into blue under daylight as strain was increased, which indicated mechanochemical activation of SP into MC in the elastomers. This was supported by the characteristic absorption peak at 560 nm of the UV–vis absorption spectra of PSP×CD and PSP + CD before and after mechanochemical activation (Figure S9). Furthermore, the color hue of PSP×CD was stronger than PSP + CD as well as the UV–vis absorption intensity at 560 nm (Figure S10), verifying the reinforcement of mechanoresponsiveness by covalent crosslinking CD nanofillers to polymer matrix.

Owing to the overlap between the emission spectrum of CDs and the fluorescent excitation of MC as shown in Fig. 4 a, polymer materials exhibited the feature of FRET [53,58]. FRET was measured during tensile tests and after UV irradiation, respectively. When 418 nm light was shed on the sample during stretching, PSP×CD turned from blue into bright purplish red with increasing strain (Fig. 4b), indicating successful FRET from CDs to MC. The mechanochromism of PSP×CD under daylight and 418 nm was then plotted into the Commission International d’Eclairage color coordinates diagram (Figure S11), so as to better reveal its color dependence on strain. The CIE diagram obviously displays the fluorescent color change from blue to purplish red with increasing strain. Conversely, stretching PSP + CD to more than 1100% under 418 nm irradiation did not show obvious purplish red fluorescence (Figure S12). It was also noted that when the load was removed the samples tuned immediately from blue into purple under daylight, but the purplish red fluorescence from PSP×CD did not show noticeable change until 60 min (Figure S13 and Figure S14). This color change under daylight upon relaxation has been attributed to the isomerization of MC to different ring-opened structures, which may have the same color of fluorescence.

Interestingly, when UV was used to activate the SP in the elastomers, only a color change into purple was observed under daylight and purplish red fluorescence was obtained under 418 nm excitation for both PSP×CD and PSP + CD (Fig. 5 a). Also, it was found that the fluorescence of the UV treated PSP + CD under 418 nm excitation was very similar to that of PSP×CD, different from the FRET after mechanochemical activation. The difference of FRET between mechanical activation and UV activation of SP is attributed to the larger fraction of MC in PSP×CD than in PSP + CD sample in tensile tests. PSP + CD with physically blended CDs is softer and more stretchable, therefore lower level of SP activation and weaker FRET is obtained in elongation tests. Fluorescent emission spectra of PSP×CD and PSP + CD under 418 nm excitation before and after mechanochemical activation are shown in Fig. 5b, together with those of PSP×CD and PSP + CD treated with UV irradiation. The original PSP×CD only exhibited the emission of CDs with a peak at around 475 nm. After mechanochemical activation (400% strain for PSP×CD and 1100% strain for PSP + CD), the intensity of the emission at 475 nm decreased and the emission at 660 nm from MC appeared. When PSP×CD was irradiated with UV light, the intensity of the emission at 475 nm decreased further and the intensity of the emission at 660 nm increased significantly. These results again validate successful FRET between CD and mechanochemically generated MC isomers in the elastomers [59]. PSP + CD also showed identical emission from MC with PSP×CD under UV irradiation, but lower FRET for PSP + CD in tensile was owing to the lower fraction of MC generated (See Fig. 5b), which was consistent with the observation in measurement. The strong FRET in PSP + CD after UV irradiation probably attributed to the high SP concentration, which shortened the physical distance between SP and CDs in bulk. To verify the hypothesis, a new polyurethane network with 1 wt% of SP concentration relative to PSP + CD was synthesized. After UV irradiation, a strong FRET phenomenon was observed (See Figure S15). According to the results of FRET, it is concluded that the covalent link between the luminescent CD nanoparticles and polymer matrix does not influence the FRET of materials after UV activation, but it improves the mechanical properties and mechanical activation of SP. The resulting higher level of SP activation further enhances FRET in tensile tests. Accordingly, FRET can then be tailored by varying the density of covalent crosslinking between CD nanoparticles and polymer chains.

Inspired by the excellent mechanochromism and photochromism of PSP×CD, we decided to explore the application in rewritable mechanochemical and photochemical recording or printing. Fig. 6 (left) is the mechanochemically printed patterns by compressing the PSP×CD film with a stamp under daylight and 418 nm, respectively. With the use of photomask, UV light irradiation also gave printed patterns on the film (Fig. 6, right). Taking advantage of the FRET property, the color of the printed patterns could be tuned by switching between daylight and 418 nm. In addition, the mechanochromism and photochromism are fully reversible. Illuminating by bright white light can readily recover the original color and fluorescence. Hence, the printed patterns can be erased quickly by using bright white light irradiation. Accordingly, the results made the as-prepared PSP×CD elastomer promising multi-functional responsive material for sensing and recording.

In summary, we have developed a mechanochromic polyurethane elastomer carrying spiropyran mechanophores in the chain and chemically crosslinked by luminescent CDs. This CD crosslinked elastomer exhibited excellent color changes under stretching. The CD nanoparticle crosslinks acted as nanofillers and robust energy donor to reinforce the mechanochromism via nanocomposites and FRET mechanism. On the contrary, the control with physically blended CDs and weaker mechanical properties showed much less effective FRET due to lower level of SP activation in stretching. Our study shows that interfacial chemical bonding plays a critical role in the reinforcement of mechanical properties of polymer nanocomposites and the FRET capacity of materials can be tailored by adjusting mechanical properties of materials. Moreover, the CD crosslinked elastomer has been demonstrated to show great potential for uses in mechanochemically and photochemically recordings.

Wenli Gao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Experiments, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Shishuai Xiang: Investigation, Validation, Supervision. Minghao Bai: Investigation, Validation, Supervision. Yonghong Ruan: Investigation, Supervision. Jinli Zheng: Investigation, Supervision. Xuezheng Cao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yuanze Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yinjun Chen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wengui Weng: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774106 and No. 11974291).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2022.125278.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spatially selective and density-controlled activation of interfacial mechanophores

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.R. Sulkanen, J. Sung, M.J. Robb, J.S. Moore, N.R. Sottos, G.-y. Liu, Spatially selective and density-controlled activation of interfacial mechanophores, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 141(9) (2019) 4080-4085.
Y. Yuan, W. Yuan, Y. Chen, Recent advances in mechanoluminescent polymers, Sci. China Mater. 59(6) (2016) 507-520.
Y. Chen, G. Mellot, D. van Luijk, C. Creton, R.P. Sijbesma, Mechanochemical tools for polymer materials, Chem. Soc. Rev. 50(6) (2021) 4100-4140.
